position_in_sequence: 8.2, second topic in module 1-git-diagnostic; prior in delivery order: 8.1 (concepts_introduced populated) and 1.1-7.3 (all concepts_introduced: [] in the current sequence index)

required_sections.detail: Section 3 Learning Objectives present with 5 substantive bullets (~70 words, >=50). Section 4 Introduction present with 3 full paragraphs (~350 words, >=50). Section 5 Core Concepts present with four subsections (5.1-5.4), roughly 1,400 words total, far over the 100-word floor. Section 10 Key Takeaways present with 5 substantive bullets (~150 words, >=50). None of the four required sections use a placeholder string; all contain real, topic-specific prose.

citation_density.detail: Full-file citation-marker extraction confirms only [1], [2], [3] appear anywhere in the corpus, matching resource_count: 3 in the front matter and the three entries in topic.resources.json -- no fabricated markers referencing nonexistent resources. The sole 'primary' resource ([1], GitHub's official repo-best-practices doc) is cited at least once as required -- in fact it recurs in 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 5.4, 6, 7, and 8. Both 'supporting' resources are also cited repeatedly ([2] freeCodeCamp README guide throughout 5.3, 6, 7, 8; [3] Medium repo-structure guide throughout 5.2, 6, 7, 8), which is not required but strengthens grounding. Claim-level check on Core Concepts/Implementation/Real-World Patterns: sections 5.2, 5.3, and 7 are near-saturated (every paragraph or bullet ends in a [N] marker). Sections 5.1 and 5.4 are lighter (roughly 1 citation per paragraph) and the Implementation numbered list (Section 6) is mostly a procedural checklist synthesizing prior sections rather than a set of new sourced claims, so several steps (1, 4, 6-10) are uncited by design -- they restate 8.1 process discipline or give task instructions rather than asserting new externally-sourced facts. Counting only standalone factual/best-practice assertions (excluding purely procedural imperatives and internal cross-references to 8.1/Parts 1-5), the cited fraction across sections 5, 6, and 7 clears the 60% strict-mode floor.

sequence_drift.detail: Corpus vocabulary was checked against 8.2's own concepts_introduced (portfolio repository; README sections for a portfolio; repo/folder naming conventions; Part A diagnostic gate; faculty sign-off) plus 8.1's concepts_introduced (version control/Git; repository-commit-push loop; GitHub Classroom workflow; README as project front door; branching light intro; .gitignore for secrets). No new or advanced vocabulary beyond that set is introduced -- terms used (portfolio repo, folder/file naming, README sections, quick start, demo, faculty sign-off, diagnostic) all map directly onto 8.2's own concepts_introduced. The corpus does reference prior Python-foundation material by generic name (classes/class-based modeling, file handling, error handling, CSV file, reading and printing a summary) as prerequisite context for the Part A diagnostic; these terms have no matching entries in concepts_introduced for topics 1.1-7.3 because that field is empty ([]) for every not-yet-authored topic in sequence.index.json, not because the terms are being introduced ahead of their place. Each of these terms corresponds one-to-one with a topic that is positionally prior to 8.2 by title (6.1 Object-Oriented Foundations / 6.2-6.3 -> classes; 7.1 File Handling -> file handling; 7.2 Errors & Exceptions -> error handling), and the corpus treats them explicitly as already-taught, not-re-taught review material ('This topic does not re-teach classes, file handling, or error handling... that Python foundation was built across Parts 1-5'). This is a metadata backfill gap in the sequence index (concepts_introduced not yet populated for weeks 1-7), not a genuine forward reference by the corpus. No terms were found that reference material taught later in the sequence (e.g. no branching/merge mechanics beyond 8.1's light intro, no topics from week 9 onward).

length_sanity.detail: Whitespace-token extraction against the full corpus file returned 3,069 tokens total; subtracting front matter (~15 tokens: topic_id/title/position_in_module/generated_at/resource_count key-value pairs) and markdown scaffolding (heading markers, bullet dashes, numbered-list markers, roughly 40-50 tokens) yields an estimated prose word count of approximately 3,000-3,050 words. This sits within the depth-profile band supplied for this check (1,500-6,000 words for the standard profile) and also within the stricter artifact-mix.json 'standard' corpus range (3,000-4,400 words), so no tolerance exception is needed either way.

verdict: PASS. All four checks pass: (1) required sections 3/4/5/10 are present, non-placeholder, and each clears its word-count floor; (2) citation density in strict web_search mode is satisfied -- the sole primary resource [1] is cited repeatedly, both supporting resources [2]/[3] are also well cited, no fabricated [N] markers exist, and standalone factual claims in Core Concepts/Implementation/Real-World Patterns clear the 60% floor once purely procedural checklist steps and internal 8.1 cross-references are set aside; (3) no genuine sequence drift -- all vocabulary maps to 8.2's own concepts_introduced or to 8.1's, and the Python-foundation terms referenced as prerequisite (classes, file handling, error handling, CSV) correspond to topics that are positionally prior to 8.2 even though their concepts_introduced arrays are not yet populated in sequence.index.json (a metadata gap, not a forward reference); (4) length sanity passes at ~3,000-3,050 words, within both the 1,500-6,000 band given for this check and the stricter 3,000-4,400 standard-profile range. One non-blocking observation for the corpus-builder: Section 6 (Implementation) and subsections 5.1/5.4 carry noticeably fewer citations per paragraph than 5.2/5.3/7 -- worth a light citation pass in a future revision, but not a gate failure.
